epigraph.text: The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork.
epigraph.quote: It is strange that astronomers have not drawn attention to the remarkable character of certain facts.

content.text:
Knowing the center of a distribution tells only half the story. Two 
distributions can have the same mean but very different spreads. A 
complete description requires measures of variability.
The simplest measure of spread is the **range**.

content.title: Range
content.content:
The **range** of a dataset is the difference between the largest 
and smallest observations:
\[
 \text{Range} = \text{Maximum} - \text{Minimum}
\]

content.text:
The range is easy to compute but uses only two values, making it 
extremely sensitive to outliers.

content.title: Percentiles and Quartiles
content.content:
The **$p$th percentile** is a value such that 
$p\%$ of the observations fall at or below it.

The **quartiles** divide the data into four 
equal parts:

- $Q_1$ (first quartile) = 25th percentile

- $Q_2$ (second quartile) = median = 50th percentile

- $Q_3$ (third quartile) = 75th percentile

content.text:
To find quartiles: first locate the median, then find the median of 
the lower half (for $Q_1$) and the median of the upper half (for $Q_3$).

content.title: Five-Number Summary
content.content:
The **five-number summary** of a 
dataset consists of:
\[
 \text{Minimum}, \quad Q_1, \quad \text{Median}, \quad Q_3, \quad \text{Maximum}
\]

content.text:
A **boxplot** (or box-and-whisker plot) displays 
the five-number summary graphically:

items: A line inside the box marks the median
"Whiskers" extend to the minimum and maximum

content.title: Constructing a Boxplot
content.problem:
The ages of 12 participants in a study are: 22, 25, 27, 29, 31, 34, 
36, 38, 41, 45, 52, 61. Find the five-number summary and sketch a 
boxplot.
content.solution:
**Minimum:** 22 **Maximum:** 61

**Median:** With $n = 12$, the median is the average of positions 
6 and 7: $\text{Median} = \frac{34 + 36}{2} = 35$

**$Q_1$:** Median of lower half (22, 25, 27, 29, 31, 34) is 
$\frac{27 + 29}{2} = 28$

**$Q_3$:** Median of upper half (36, 38, 41, 45, 52, 61) is 
$\frac{41 + 45}{2} = 43$

Five-number summary: 22, 28, 35, 43, 61

The boxplot shows the distribution is slightly skewed right, with 
the upper whisker longer than the lower.

content.title: Interquartile Range
content.content:
The **interquartile range** is
\[
 \text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1
\]
The IQR measures the spread of the middle 50\% of the data.

content.text:
The IQR is resistant to outliers since it ignores the extreme 25\% 
on each end.
A common rule for identifying potential outliers:

content.title: The 1.5 $\times$ IQR Rule
content.content:
An observation is a suspected **outlier** if it 
falls more than $1.5 \times \text{IQR}$ below $Q_1$ or above $Q_3$.

Lower fence: $Q_1 - 1.5 \times \text{IQR}$

Upper fence: $Q_3 + 1.5 \times \text{IQR}$

content.text:
**Modified boxplots** show whiskers extending to the most extreme 
non-outlier values, with outliers plotted as individual points.

The most important measures of spread are based on deviations from 
the mean.

content.title: Sample Variance and Standard Deviation
content.content:
The **sample variance** of observations 
$x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ with mean $\xbar$ is
\[
 s^2 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \xbar)^2}{n-1}
\]

The **sample standard deviation** is
\[
 s = \sqrt{s^2} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \xbar)^2}{n-1}}
\]

content.text:
Why $n-1$ instead of $n$? We lose one "degree of freedom" because 
we use $\xbar$ (estimated from the data) rather than the true 
population mean $\mu$. This adjustment makes $s^2$ an unbiased 
estimator of the population variance $\sigma^2$.

content.title: Computing Variance and Standard Deviation
content.solution:
First, find the mean: $\xbar = \frac{4+7+9+12+13}{5} = 9$

Next, compute deviations and squared deviations:

| $x_i$ | $x_i - \xbar$ | $(x_i - \xbar)^2$ |
| 4 | $-5$ | 25 |
| 7 | $-2$ | 4 |
| 9 | 0 | 0 |
| 12 | 3 | 9 |
| 13 | 4 | 16 |
| Sum: | 54 |

\[
 s^2 = \frac{54}{5-1} = \frac{54}{4} = 13.5
\]
\[
 s = \sqrt{13.5} \approx 3.67
\]

items: $s \geq 0$, with $s = 0$ only when all observations are identical.
$s$ has the same units as the original data.

The mean and standard deviation are natural partners; use them 
 together for symmetric distributions.

content.title: Which Spread Measure to Use?
content.content:
- For **symmetric distributions without outliers**: use 
 the standard deviation $s$ (paired with the mean $\xbar$).

- For **skewed distributions or data with outliers**: use 
 the IQR (paired with the median).

marginNotes.text:
Unequal weights and unequal measures 
are both alike an abomination to the Lord.
